gist: Osmo founder/CEO Alex Wilsko explains how his team solved the century-old 'structure-odor-relation' problem with a ~300-dimensional 'principal odor map' embedding, and is using a self-funding fragrance-formulation business (blending existing approved ingredients, likened to 'Canva for scent') to bankroll a larger bet: an olfactory-intelligence 'fleet of models' trained on 6 billion enumerated molecules and 5.43 million human sniffs, aimed eventually at disease detection once data-scarcity and business-model barriers are solved. Despite the Finance/Tech section tag, this is a pure AI/biotech science interview with no market, ticker, or macro commentary — insights below are the transcript's actual technical and business content, framed for investor relevance.

themes.theme: Digitizing Smell: The Core AI Problem

insights.claim: Osmo's founding technical insight is that giving computers a sense of smell requires solving three separate steps — reading (turning molecules into data), mapping (encoding smell into a manipulable digital representation like RGB did for color), and writing (reproducing scent physically) — and the 'map' step, already solved decades ago for vision and audio, was the missing piece for smell.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: When evaluating 'AI for physical senses' startups, ask which of the three steps (read/map/write) they've actually solved — most value accrues to whoever owns the map/embedding layer.
insights.quote: So the thing we focused on at Google Brain was the missing piece, which is for scent is the map.

insights.claim: The human nose has over 300 distinct types of olfactory receptors versus roughly three-to-four color channels (RGB-equivalent) in the human eye, making smell a much higher-dimensional sensory modality than vision, even though scientists don't yet know exactly what each receptor type encodes.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: The dimensionality gap explains why AI's vision/language breakthroughs haven't transferred to smell and signals the modeling problem is fundamentally harder — and more defensible — to solve.
insights.quote: There's three channels of color information roughly roughly RGB uh in the eye, but there's over 300 channels of alactory information in the nose.

insights.claim: The 'structure-odor relationship' problem — predicting what a molecule smells like purely from its chemical structure — went unsolved for 100 years and was widely believed unsolvable until Osmo's team trained a graph neural network on structure-to-descriptor pairs and validated it in a blind 'odor Turing test,' where the model's predictions matched or beat the average human panelist.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: This benchmark win is the technical proof point that de-risks the entire olfactory-AI category for investors.
insights.quote: That problem called the structure odor relation problem had been unsolved for a hundred years and actually in some cases people thought it was unsolvable.

insights.claim: When Osmo's ~300-dimensional neural embedding of molecular smell was reduced to two dimensions via PCA and plotted for about 5,000 molecules, scents self-organized into nested, biologically coherent neighborhoods (e.g., floral splitting into jasmine/rose/violet sub-regions; fermented/alcoholic scents clustering into a shape resembling a bottle) without being explicitly labeled with that hierarchy.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Emergent, unsupervised structure in the embedding is evidence the 'principal odor map' is a real perceptual coordinate system rather than curve-fit noise — a potentially durable technical moat.
insights.quote: the region for um for fermented and alcoholic was actually shaped like a bottle during our first model train and we haven't touched it since because it's so funny.

insights.claim: Molecules that carry a smell are physically constrained to roughly 3 to 20 atoms, because larger molecules are too heavy to become airborne and won't fit into the binding pockets of olfactory receptors, which is why Osmo's graph neural networks operate on small molecular graphs rather than large-scale structures.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Bounds the problem's compute/search space — part of why olfactory AI became tractable now with modern ML rather than requiring exotic new architectures.

insights.claim: Osmo has digitized 6 billion candidate molecules by enumerating all chemically realizable, manufacturable structures within physical constraints (roughly 3-20 atoms, valid bond types) and running predictive smell/safety models across the entire set, rather than only working with molecules already found in nature.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A combinatorial molecule library at this scale functions as a proprietary search space that's hard for competitors to replicate without similar infrastructure.

insights.claim: Osmo has collected 5.43 million human 'sniffs' (trained human scent labels), which it claims is the largest olfactory dataset ever assembled, and had to build the entire labeling pipeline in-house because no equivalent of Scale AI or Mechanical Turk exists for smell.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Because no outsourced data-labeling market exists for this modality, Osmo's in-house data infrastructure is a genuine structural moat rather than something a well-funded competitor could simply buy.
insights.quote: there's no scale AI or there's no mechanical Turk for smell. like we've had to make that internally, consume it ourselves and generate huge amounts of alactory data for alactory intelligence.

insights.claim: Osmo built its commercial fragrance-design business so that every paid customer project also generates fresh proprietary sensor and human-panel scent data, creating a self-funding flywheel where revenue and data collection reinforce rather than trade off against each other.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: The go-to-market also pays for the R&D dataset, reducing cash-burn risk versus pure research-stage olfactory-AI competitors — a capital-efficiency thesis worth flagging in any deep-tech AI diligence.
insights.quote: it's creates this really nice like self-perpetuating machine where we found a business where we can serve the world

insights.claim: Alex Wilsko argues the real competitive moat in olfactory AI is not the current size of a dataset but the rate of new data generation, dismissing century-old fragrance/flavor incumbents' claims of proprietary data as mostly unusable legacy records.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: For diligence on incumbents entering 'AI for scent,' the rate of structured data generation — not years-in-business or legacy archives — is the metric to underwrite.
insights.quote: even the companies have been around for hundred years, like they say they have data, but they have is a lot of Excel spreadsheets that don't actually kind of add up to much.

themes.theme: From Lab to Factory: Osmo's Fragrance Business Model

insights.claim: Osmo's primary near-term revenue comes not from inventing entirely new fragrance molecules but from using AI to blend existing, already regulatory-approved ingredients into new scent formulas on demand for brand customers (e.g., shampoo, air freshener launches), positioning the product as software akin to 'Canva or Figma for scent.'
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: The near-term commercial model is a lower-regulatory-risk, faster-time-to-market formulation tool built on commodity ingredients rather than a slow novel-chemical-approval business — relevant to modeling revenue ramp speed.
insights.quote: you can think of that as like a Canva, right? Or or a Figma for scent.

insights.claim: Osmo's factory can decode a target point in its scent embedding space back into a physical formula and produce a new fragrance roughly every 100 seconds using equipment the size of a school bus.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A concrete throughput figure for modeling Osmo's manufacturing capacity and scalability against traditional perfumery development timelines.
insights.quote: a big robot that's the size of a school bus that can make a new fragrance every hundred seconds.

insights.claim: Axe body spray is cited as a historical case study of bringing fine-fragrance-grade scent molecules to a mass-market price point for the first time, illustrating that new scent design typically evolves incrementally from existing scent lineages rather than making sudden jumps.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Useful framing for evaluating 'AI-generated fragrance' claims — expect evolutionary, derivative outputs from these models rather than category-redefining novel scents in the near term.
insights.quote: Axe is actually really famous because it was the first time that fine fragrance perfumes were actually brought to that mass market price point.

insights.claim: New molecules are sometimes developed specifically as safety- or regulation-driven replacements for existing fragrance ingredients being phased out, not purely for novel scent creation — for example, needing an optically clear vanilla-note substitute because natural vanilla is brown and brands want colorless formulations.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Regulatory-driven ingredient replacement is a recurring, defensible revenue category versus discretionary innovation spend — relevant to estimating demand durability for Osmo's molecule pipeline.

themes.theme: Beyond Fragrance: Disease Detection and the Miniaturization Roadmap

insights.claim: Alex Wilsko believes disease detection via smell (early cancer or malaria detection) is scientifically plausible — since dogs can already be trained to detect such patterns — but is currently blocked by pure data scarcity: there aren't enough sick and healthy human subjects available to train a reliable model on subtle, distributed molecular signals.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Frames medical-scent-diagnostics feasibility as gated primarily by large-scale human data-collection logistics, not algorithm quality — a key diligence question for that sub-sector.
insights.quote: we need to like band together and build a huge effort where we just like let's go sniff 10,000 people.

insights.claim: Despite scientific promise, Alex Wilsko states medical diagnostics is 'not a great business to be in,' which he says is the primary reason Osmo has focused its first four years on the fragrance industry rather than disease-scent detection, even though the underlying science and technology largely overlap.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A direct insider signal that scent-based medical diagnostics may be scientifically tractable but commercially unattractive standalone — a red flag to weigh against any pitch that leads with the medical use case.
insights.quote: Medical diagnostics are like not a great unfortunately not a great business to be in.

insights.claim: Osmo already has a deployable chemical-sensing device, about the size of two shoe boxes, that the company claims performs as well as a trained human nose, though shrinking it to phone-chip scale will require dedicated engineering investment not yet prioritized due to unclear near-term business models for consumer/medical smell sensing.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A concrete current-state benchmark (shoe-box scale, human-parity performance) for timing any 'olfactory sensor on a chip' hardware thesis.
insights.quote: We have a deployable sensor that's as good as our nose as you and me. Um and it's the size of two shoe boxes.

insights.claim: Wilsko compares today's chemical/olfactory sensing to 1970s mainframe computing — powerful but confined to specialized lab environments — and expects the same miniaturization trajectory that took computing from mainframes to phones to eventually apply to scent sensors.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Sets a long-horizon (likely decade-plus) expectation for consumer olfactory hardware, useful for timing any hardware-dependent investment thesis in this space.
insights.quote: we're still in the data center era of chemical sensing and of all factory intelligence.

insights.claim: Wilsko expects that if chip-scale olfactory sensors reach consumer devices, resulting applications (food spoilage detection, personal scent tracking, novelty apps) will likely be as unpredictable and broad as the range of apps that emerged once smartphone cameras became ubiquitous, drawing on roughly 80 known dog-detectable scent tasks as a rough application inventory.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Analogous-technology framing (camera-phone app explosion) for sizing a future TAM for consumer olfactory sensing hardware.

themes.theme: Model Architecture Philosophy: Fleet of Models vs. End-to-End

insights.claim: Osmo's 'olfactory intelligence' is not a single monolithic foundation model but a fleet of dozens of specialized predictive models (odor character, intensity, safety/toxicity, manufacturability, cost) coordinated together — an architecture Wilsko says is philosophically closer to how autonomous-vehicle stacks are built than to a single end-to-end model.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A useful architectural precedent for physical-world/multi-sensor AI startups — expect fleets of narrow specialized models rather than a single scaled foundation model in early-stage physical-AI domains.
insights.quote: the way we think about it is closer to autonomous vehicles which is actually where my CTO's kind of background is.

insights.claim: Osmo explicitly does not optimize its models for physical/mechanistic faithfulness to real olfactory biology; it optimizes purely for predictive accuracy, using physics-based modeling only when it happens to be the most useful modeling choice for a given sub-problem.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Signals a pragmatic, empiricist ML philosophy rather than a mechanistic one — affects how explainable or transferable its models may be to adjacent domains like taste.
insights.quote: we don't necessarily strive for physical understanding. We strive for predictive accuracy.

insights.claim: Because regulatory approval requires specific, isolable safety predictions across multiple dimensions (skin safety, inhalation safety, aquatic toxicity, etc.), Osmo cannot fully consolidate its models into one unified system and instead maintains multiple outputs/heads that function as effectively independent models even where architecture is shared.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Regulatory requirements are directly shaping model architecture in this space — a structural constraint that will persist and limit how 'unified' foundation models can become in regulated physical-product domains.

insights.claim: Wilsko predicts that future foundation-model 'model cards' will eventually include benchmarks for smell and taste prediction (e.g., malaria detection, cancer detection, or scent formulation for a product launch) alongside today's text/vision/language benchmarks, once enough foundational olfactory data has been collected.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A forward-looking signal for what 'multimodal AI' benchmarking could expand to — investors tracking foundation-model roadmaps should watch for scent/taste benchmarks as an emerging category marker.
insights.quote: malaria detection will be a benchmark all right and cancer detection will be benchmark and you know building a great um market product for a new shampoo launch in Malaysia will be a benchmark.

themes.theme: The Copernican View: Rethinking Intelligence Beyond Human Language

insights.claim: Wilsko argues that because 99% of species on Earth (bacteria, fungi, plants, insects) communicate only through chemistry, AI models trained solely on human-generated text and images are missing an entire class of 'alien' intelligence, and he wants olfactory AI to be explicitly trained on the chemical signaling produced by non-human life as a distinct form of intellectual output.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A novel framing for what 'multimodal AI' could include beyond human-derived data — relevant to thinking about data moats built from non-anthropocentric signal sources.
insights.quote: 99% of species on this planet can only speak with chemistry, right?

insights.claim: Citing mathematician Terry Tao's writing on needing a 'Copernican view of intelligence' — not placing human intelligence at the center of the AI debate — Wilsko frames Osmo's long-term mission as building AI systems informed by non-human forms of intelligence and communication, not just replicating or exceeding human cognition.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Helps distinguish Osmo's stated long-term ambition (broadening what AI models capture beyond human intelligence) from its current fragrance-business cash cow — relevant to assessing mission scope versus current monetization.
insights.quote: I think increasingly we need to have a capernac view of intelligence.

themes.theme: Human Perception, Emotion, and Skepticism of Hype

insights.claim: Wilsko states the widely cited claim that humans can distinguish 'a trillion' distinct smells has been 'pretty thoroughly debunked,' and instead argues humans are highly sensitive smellers — able to detect some molecules at parts-per-billion or -trillion concentrations, as with the mercaptan additive used to give natural gas its smell — even though there's no reliable estimate of the total number of discriminable smells.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Corrects a commonly repeated pop-science statistic sometimes used to hype olfactory-AI market-size claims — a useful fact-check when evaluating other companies' marketing in this space.
insights.quote: There is a very famous paper which has a trillion which has been pretty thoroughly debunked.

insights.claim: Wilsko is skeptical of the 'neurosense' trend that uses EEG devices to infer emotional states, arguing EEG is only reliably validated for detecting sleep and seizures, not measuring mood or emotional valence, and that Osmo has deliberately avoided entering emotion-detection products until it can do so rigorously.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: A direct insider critique of 'neurotech for emotion' claims — a useful red flag when evaluating startups selling EEG-based emotional-state products.
insights.quote: it's not going to tell you if you're feeling, you know, up or low, right? It's just it's not can't do that. There's no signal there.

insights.claim: Wilsko personally believes traditional practices like aromatherapy and Ayurveda contain a real, physiologically grounded kernel of truth about scent's effects on mood and focus, comparing this to how acupuncture's underlying mechanisms were later validated by the Western practice of 'dry needling,' while acknowledging current research on scent-emotion links isn't yet clear-cut.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Signals a plausible future R&D/product direction (scientifically validated aromatherapy-style scent-mood products) that Osmo isn't yet pursuing but considers credible — a potential future product line to watch.

insights.claim: Flavor perception is roughly 90% smell and only 10% taste, demonstrated by the effect that holding one's nose while eating a flavored jelly bean makes it impossible to distinguish flavors like lemon, lime, or grapefruit, even though basic taste qualities (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) remain perceptible.
insights.speaker: Alex Wilsko, Osmo founder & CEO
insights.so_what: Relevant baseline fact for evaluating any 'AI flavor' or food-tech venture — most perceived flavor differentiation is olfactory, not gustatory, meaning scent-AI capabilities like Osmo's could be directly portable to flavor/food applications.
insights.quote: flavor is 90% smell, 10% taste.
